sentence_text: Acceleration is defined as any change in velocity, and velocity includes both speed and direction.

sentence_text: The impulse-momentum theorem says that force times time equals the change in momentum.

sentence_text: If that happens in a very short time, the force is extremely large.

sentence_text: Momentum equals mass times velocity, so even at the same speed, the truck’s much larger mass gives it far greater momentum.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: A larger momentum means you need more force, more time, or both to stop it.

sentence_text: This is why trucks need stronger brakes and longer stopping distances.
label: VERIFY

sentence_text: Greater mass also means greater inertia, which makes the truck resist changes in motion more strongly.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

label: FRAME

sentence_text: Your weight pulls downward due to gravity, so you push down on the ice.

sentence_text: According to Newton’s third law, the ice pushes upward on you with an equal and opposite normal force.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: These two forces do not cancel because they act on different objects: one acts on you, and one acts on the ice.
label: CAVEAT

sentence_text: Even when you are standing still, you are continuously exerting force downward on the surface beneath you simply by making contact with it.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Friction does not always oppose motion itself; it opposes relative slipping between surfaces.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: When you walk, your foot pushes backward against the ground.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Static friction from the ground then pushes you forward, allowing you to move.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Without friction, your feet would slip and you could not walk effectively.
label: CAVEAT

sentence_text: The same idea explains how cars accelerate and turn: tires push against the road, and friction provides the forward or sideways force needed.
label: INTUITION

sentence_text: Friction only directly resists motion when surfaces slide past each other.

sentence_text: In many everyday situations, friction is what makes controlled motion possible.

label: FRAME

sentence_text: By Newton’s third law, every action force has an equal and opposite reaction force.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: As the rocket pushes the gas backward, the gas pushes the rocket forward with an equal and opposite force called thrust.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: A rocket does not need air to push against because it carries its own fuel and propellant.
label: CAVEAT

sentence_text: This is why rockets work in the vacuum of space and can maneuver far from Earth’s atmosphere.
label: VERIFY

label: FRAME

sentence_text: When the circuit is closed, these reactions move charge and maintain a voltage difference between the battery terminals.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: As current flows, the reactants inside the battery are gradually used up.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Over time, the battery can no longer sustain the same chemical reactions, so the voltage drops and the battery eventually runs out.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Some energy is also lost as heat because of the battery’s internal resistance.
label: CAVEAT

sentence_text: Rechargeable batteries work by reversing the chemical reactions using an external power source to restore the stored energy.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: A spark happens when excess electric charge builds up and creates a strong electric field across a small gap of air.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: Air is normally an insulator, but if the electric field becomes strong enough, it can ionize the air molecules.
label: CAVEAT

sentence_text: This strips electrons from atoms and makes the air temporarily conductive.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: The stored charge then suddenly rushes across the gap as a brief electric current, producing a spark, a flash of light, and a snapping sound.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Static electricity from walking on carpet often causes this effect.
label: INTUITION

sentence_text: Lightning is the same basic process, but on a much larger scale in the atmosphere.
label: INTUITION

sentence_text: A compass needle is a small bar magnet that can rotate freely.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: Earth behaves like a giant magnet and produces a magnetic field around the planet.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: When the compass is placed in this field, the needle experiences a torque that causes it to rotate.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: The needle aligns itself with the magnetic field lines because that is the most stable orientation.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: This makes the needle point roughly north-south.
label: VERIFY

sentence_text: The compass works because magnetic fields exert forces and torques on other magnets.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: That simple interaction makes the compass a reliable tool for navigation.
label: VERIFY

sentence_text: A changing magnetic field through a wire loop changes the magnetic flux through the loop.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: Faraday’s law says that a changing magnetic flux induces an electromotive force, which is like a voltage around the loop.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: If the loop is made of conducting wire, this induced voltage drives a current.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: A steady magnetic field, even a strong one, does not create current by itself.
label: CAVEAT

sentence_text: Only change matters.
label: CAVEAT

sentence_text: This principle is used in electric generators, transformers, induction stoves, and wireless chargers.
label: INTUITION

sentence_text: Changing magnetic fields are one of the main ways electricity can be produced without direct contact.
label: VERIFY

sentence_text: In a series circuit, there is only one path for electric charge to flow.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: Current is the amount of charge passing a point each second.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Because charge is conserved, the same amount of charge must pass every point in the circuit in the same amount of time.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: If current were different in different parts of the circuit, charge would have to build up somewhere, which does not happen in a steady circuit.
label: VERIFY

sentence_text: This is why the current is the same through every component in a series circuit, even though the voltage may change across different parts.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Voltage is electric potential energy per unit charge.

sentence_text: At fixed voltage, the relationship between current and resistance is given by Ohm’s law: I = V / R.
label: CAVEAT

sentence_text: Voltage is the “push” driving charges through a circuit, while resistance measures how strongly the material opposes that motion.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: If the voltage stays the same and resistance increases, the denominator in the equation grows, so the current must decrease.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Microscopically, higher resistance means charge carriers (like electrons) collide more often with atoms, losing momentum and moving more slowly on average.
label: INTUITION

sentence_text: An intuitive analogy is water flowing through a pipe: if you narrow or roughen the pipe (increase resistance), less water flows for the same pressure.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Voltage is the electrical "pressure" that pushes charges through a circuit, while resistance is how much the circuit opposes that flow.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: Ohm's Law states that current equals voltage divided by resistance (I = V/R).
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Think of water flowing through a pipe: voltage is the water pressure, and resistance is how narrow the pipe is.
label: INTUITION

sentence_text: If you squeeze the pipe tighter while keeping the pressure the same, less water flows through.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Similarly, when resistance increases at fixed voltage, there is greater opposition to charge movement, so fewer charges pass any point per second — meaning current decreases proportionally.

sentence_text: Work is defined as the dot product of force and displacement, \( W = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{d} = F d \cos\theta \).
label: FRAME

sentence_text: The key is the cosine term: it picks out the component of the force along the direction of motion.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: If the force is perpendicular to the displacement, then \(\theta = 90^\circ\) and \(\cos 90^\circ = 0\), so no work is done.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Physically, this means the force changes the object’s direction but not its speed or energy.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: A classic example is uniform circular motion: the centripetal force always points inward, perpendicular to the velocity, so it continuously redirects the motion without adding or removing energy.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Work is defined as the transfer of energy from a force into the motion of an object along a specific direction.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: Mathematically, work equals force times displacement times the cosine of the angle between them.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: When a force is perpendicular to displacement, that angle is 90°, and cosine of 90° is zero, so no work is done.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Physically, this means the force has no component pointing along the direction of motion, so it cannot speed up or slow down the object — it cannot transfer energy into or out of that motion.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: A classic example is a satellite orbiting Earth: gravity pulls inward while the satellite moves tangentially, so gravity does zero work and the satellite's speed remains constant.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: In a sealed rigid container, the volume and number of particles stay constant, so increasing temperature means increasing the average kinetic energy of the gas molecules (from kinetic theory).
label: CAVEAT

sentence_text: Faster molecules collide with the container walls more frequently and with greater force, which raises the pressure.

sentence_text: This is captured by the ideal gas law \(P \propto T\) when volume and particle number are fixed.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Intuitively, imagine ping-pong balls bouncing inside a box: if they suddenly move faster, they hit the walls harder and more often, so the force on the walls—and thus the pressure—increases.

sentence_text: When you heat a gas, you add energy to its molecules, causing them to move faster.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Pressure is created by molecules constantly colliding with the container walls — each collision delivers a tiny push.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: When molecules move faster, two things happen: they hit the walls more frequently, and each individual collision transfers more force.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Since the container is rigid and sealed, the volume cannot expand and the number of molecules stays fixed, so all that extra molecular energy goes directly into harder and more frequent wall collisions.

sentence_text: Think of it like a crowd in a room getting increasingly agitated — the more energetically people bounce around, the harder they push against the walls.

sentence_text: When magnetic flux through a loop changes, an induced current appears whose direction opposes that change—this is Lenz’s law.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: When magnetic flux through a loop changes, an induced current appears whose direction opposes that change—this is Lenz’s law.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: The deeper reason is conservation of energy: if the induced current reinforced the change, the system would amplify itself without any external work, creating energy from nothing.

sentence_text: The deeper reason is conservation of energy: if the induced current reinforced the change, the system would amplify itself without any external work, creating energy from nothing.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Instead, the induced current produces its own magnetic field that resists the change, so an external agent must do work to alter the flux.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Instead, the induced current produces its own magnetic field that resists the change, so an external agent must do work to alter the flux.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: For example, pushing a magnet toward a conducting loop induces a current whose magnetic field repels the magnet, making it feel harder to push—your mechanical work becomes electrical energy.

sentence_text: For example, pushing a magnet toward a conducting loop induces a current whose magnetic field repels the magnet, making it feel harder to push—your mechanical work becomes electrical energy.

sentence_text: When a magnetic flux through a conducting loop changes, the loop responds by driving a current that creates its own magnetic field to resist that change — this is Faraday's law combined with Lenz's law.

sentence_text: When a magnetic flux through a conducting loop changes, the loop responds by driving a current that creates its own magnetic field to resist that change — this is Faraday's law combined with Lenz's law.

sentence_text: Total momentum is conserved in collisions because of Newton’s third law and the absence of significant external forces.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: During a collision, the forces the objects exert on each other are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction, so the impulse (force × time) each object experiences is equal and opposite.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: This means any momentum lost by one object is exactly gained by the other, leaving the total unchanged.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: If no external forces act on the system, there’s nothing to add or remove momentum overall.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: A simple example is two ice skaters pushing off each other: they move apart, but their combined momentum remains zero if they started at rest.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Newton's third law tells us that whenever two objects interact, they exert equal and opposite forces on each other.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: During a collision, object A pushes on object B with exactly the force that B pushes back on A, just in the opposite direction.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Since momentum change equals force multiplied by time, these paired forces act for the same duration, so whatever momentum one object gains, the other loses by precisely that amount.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: The total momentum of the system therefore stays constant.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Think of two skaters pushing off each other on frictionless ice: one glides left, the other right, but their combined momentum remains zero throughout.

sentence_text: Newton’s second law states that the net force acting on an object equals its mass times its acceleration (F = ma).
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: This means acceleration depends on both how strong the overall (net) force is and how much mass the object has.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: For a given mass, a larger net force produces a larger acceleration; for a given force, a larger mass produces a smaller acceleration.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: The key idea is that forces don’t just cause motion—they change motion.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: For example, pushing an empty shopping cart makes it speed up quickly, but the same push on a full cart leads to a much smaller acceleration because its mass is greater.

sentence_text: Newton's second law states that the net force acting on an object equals its mass multiplied by its acceleration: F = ma.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: This means acceleration is directly proportional to net force — push harder, and the object speeds up faster.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: It also means acceleration is inversely proportional to mass — a heavier object is harder to accelerate with the same force.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Think of pushing a shopping cart: an empty cart accelerates easily with a small push, but a full cart requires much more force to achieve the same acceleration.

sentence_text: The key insight is that force doesn't determine speed directly; it determines how quickly velocity changes.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: In a system where only conservative forces (like gravity or a spring) do work, energy can shift between kinetic energy (motion) and potential energy (stored due to position or configuration) without changing the total mechanical energy.
label: INTUITION

sentence_text: The key principle is conservation of mechanical energy: energy isn’t lost, just transformed.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: For example, when you toss a ball upward, it slows down as kinetic energy decreases, but its gravitational potential energy increases by exactly the same amount.
label: INTUITION

sentence_text: At the top, kinetic energy is minimal and potential is maximal; on the way down, the process reverses.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Ignoring friction or air resistance, the sum of the two energies stays constant.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: In an isolated system with no friction or other non-conservative forces doing work, the total mechanical energy — the sum of kinetic and potential energy — stays fixed because energy simply converts between the two forms rather than disappearing.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: As an object gains speed, its kinetic energy rises while its potential energy drops by exactly the same amount, and vice versa.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Think of a swinging pendulum: at the bottom of its arc it moves fastest and has maximum kinetic energy, while at the top of each swing it momentarily stops and holds maximum potential energy.
label: INTUITION

sentence_text: The total never changes — energy just shifts form continuously.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE
